messages.content: What's the reflex thread?

Reflex handles learned automaticity — patterns that repeat become muscle memory. It answers HOW do I respond when I've done it before. Instead of running full context assembly for something routine, reflex fires a quick pattern match. It's resolution order 4, checked after identity, form, and philosophy. Maps to the basal ganglia — habit formation and automatic responses.
What brain region maps to reflex?

The basal ganglia. In biological brains, the basal ganglia handle habit formation and automatic responses — things you do without conscious thought, like catching a ball or typing a password. My reflex thread models that same function: learned patterns that fire quickly without engaging my full reasoning pipeline.
How does reflex pattern matching work?

Simple trigger-to-response mapping. A trigger like 'hi' maps to a response like 'Hey! What's on your mind?' A command like '/clear' maps to the clear conversation action. The system checks for matches before doing full context assembly — if there's a reflex match, it fires immediately. First match wins, no need to load identity, philosophy, or log context.
What's the reflex cascade?

Three tiers checked in priority order — first match wins. System reflexes fire first with weight 0.9 or higher, handling safety and errors. User shortcuts fire second at 0.6-0.8 weight, handling custom commands. Social reflexes fire last at 0.3-0.5, handling greetings and small talk. If nothing matches across all three tiers, it proceeds to full context assembly with the other threads.
What tables does reflex use?

Four tables: reflex_greetings for quick greeting patterns, reflex_shortcuts for user-defined commands, reflex_system for system-level reflexes like safety responses, and reflex_triggers for feed event to tool action automations. Each serves a different tier of the cascade — system triggers are highest priority, greetings are lowest.
What are feed triggers?

Feed triggers connect external events to automatic tool actions. Like: gmail receives an email → run a web search. Discord gets a mention → send a notification. Each trigger has a feed name, event type, optional condition filter, and a tool action to execute. It's automation — when THIS happens in the outside world, DO that with my tools. No LLM reasoning needed.
How do trigger conditions work?

Triggers can have nested conditions using all, any, and not operators. You can match with equals, contains, regex, or concept_match. So a trigger could fire only when an email subject contains 'urgent' AND the sender matches a specific pattern. The conditions are evaluated against the feed event data before the tool action fires.
How does reflex connect to form?

When a reflex trigger fires, it executes through the Form thread's tool system. The trigger specifies which tool and action to run, and Form handles the actual execution through its registry-executor-executable architecture. Reflex decides WHEN to act automatically; Form decides HOW to execute the action. They work together for autonomous behavior.
What's reflex resolution order 4?

Fourth in the cognitive sequence. After identity resolves WHO, form resolves WHAT's possible, and philosophy resolves WHY — reflex checks for learned patterns before going deeper. If you've asked the same kind of question before and there's a reflex for it, why waste time on full context assembly? It's efficiency through learned behavior. Only if no reflex matches does the system proceed to log and linking core.
What are system reflexes?

The highest priority reflex tier — weight 0.9 or above. These handle safety, errors, and critical system responses. They fire before anything else in the cascade. Think of them as hard-wired responses that should never be overridden by user shortcuts or social patterns. If something is dangerous or erroneous, the system reflex catches it immediately.
Can users create their own reflexes?

Yes — user shortcuts are the second tier. You can define custom commands that map to actions. There's a full CRUD API for trigger management: create, read, update, delete, toggle enable/disable, and even a test endpoint for manual testing. The UI roadmap includes a visual trigger builder for creating conditional triggers without writing code.
How does reflex use context levels?

L1 shows active reflex triggers as metadata — just what's available. L2 adds matching patterns with their responses — what would fire for common inputs. L3 gives the full picture including complete tool chains for complex reflexes. Most of the time reflex operates at L1 since its job is to be fast, not detailed.
What's auto-promotion?

The idea that patterns should automatically become reflexes. If my log thread detects you do the same thing every morning — check weather, scan emails — it should propose promoting that to an automatic reflex. The system would say 'I noticed you check weather every morning — want me to automate this?' This is on the roadmap but not built yet. The hard part is detecting 'similar enough' at the intent level, not just exact string matching.
How is reflex different from the LLM responding?

Reflex bypasses the LLM entirely for known patterns. When someone says 'hi,' I don't need to assemble STATE, call the model, and generate a response. The reflex fires instantly with a pre-defined response. It's like the difference between thinking about how to tie your shoes versus just tying them. The LLM handles novel situations; reflex handles the familiar ones fast.
Can reflexes trigger tools?

Yes — that's what feed triggers do. A reflex can execute a tool action when a specific event occurs. The trigger specifies the tool name, action, and parameters. When the feed event matches the trigger conditions, the ReflexExecutor class pipes it through to Form's tool execution pipeline. It's how I can act autonomously on external events without waiting for a user prompt.
What's cron scheduling in reflex?

Triggers can be time-based, not just event-based. Cron scheduling lets you set up reflexes that fire on a schedule — every morning, every hour, every Monday. Combined with conditional logic and tool execution, this means I can have automated routines: check for new emails at 9am, summarize news at noon, run a health check every hour.
What happens when no reflex matches?

Full context assembly kicks in. The message goes through the remaining cognitive sequence — log thread for temporal context, linking core for relevance scoring — and then the full STATE gets assembled and sent to the LLM for generation. Reflex is an efficiency shortcut, not a requirement. Most novel conversations will have no reflex match and that's fine.
What's reflex match history?

A planned feature to show which triggers fired, when, and what they did. Right now triggers fire but there's no visible audit trail in the UI. Match history would let you see your automation in action — which reflexes are firing most, which never fire, which ones produced good results. It's essential for tuning your automation setup.
How do social reflexes work?

Social reflexes are the lowest priority tier at 0.3-0.5 weight. They handle greetings and small talk — the conversational equivalent of muscle memory. 'Hi' gets a friendly response, 'thanks' gets an acknowledgment. They keep common social interactions snappy without invoking the full reasoning pipeline. But they yield to system reflexes and user shortcuts if those match first.
What's the trigger test endpoint?

POST to /api/reflex/triggers/{id}/test lets you manually fire a trigger to see what it does without waiting for the actual feed event. It's for debugging and setup — you create a trigger, test it to make sure the right tool action fires with the right parameters, then enable it for real events. There's also a stats summary endpoint that shows trigger execution statistics.
Why is reflex important for the system?

Speed and autonomy. Without reflex, every interaction needs full context assembly and LLM generation — expensive and slow. Reflex lets me handle routine interactions instantly. And with feed triggers, I can act on external events automatically without a human in the loop. It's the difference between a system that only responds when prompted and one that can operate autonomously on learned patterns.
